content.full_text:
January 30, 1991

FPPC

FEB 5 4:41 PM '91

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Formal Letter of Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am requesting a Formal Letter of Advice regarding a potential
conflict of interest and/or restriction of postgovernment service.
The request is for several questions as a succession of scenarios that
may allow some action on my part as I was caught unaware of recent
legislation potentially bearing on my future activities. You may
consider that an informal response is indicated, if so, please let me
know.

BACKGROUND

Since being appointed by the Governor in January 1984, I have served
as a member of the Occupational Safety and Health Standards Board
(Board) [see Labor Code § 140(a)]. This is a part-time position and I
have continued full-time employment in private industry. In May 1990
I began full-time self-employment as a private consultant in safety
and health matters. In December 1990, I asked for and received a
written confidential legal opinion from the Board's staff counsel
regarding the potential conflict of interest arising from representing
(one does not have to be an attorney at bar) one of my clients before
the Board in a variance application. Based upon that opinion I
decided to resign.

In December, I discussed the potential resignation with the Board's
Chairwoman, an attorney, the Acting Director of the Department of
Industrial Relations, and others. This discussion included the matter
of resignation timing.

On January 23, 1991 I wrote a letter of resignation to Governor Pete
Wilson indicating January 31, 1991 as the effective date of
resignation. The compelling reason was to avoid a conflict of
interest in representing my clients in variance applications and other
matters.

On January 24, 1991 I learned of legislation recently enacted that
deals with postgovernment restrictions. Again I sought information
from the Board's staff counsel. I was directed to Government Code §
87406(d)(1). Had I known of this provision earlier, I would have
resigned before January 7, 1991. My entire intent was to be
forthright in avoiding a conflict of interest and accommodate some
sense of harmony in the administration change period.

Letter of Advice for David W. Smith
January 30, 1991
Page 2

Since January 7, 1991 I have served two days in an official Board
capacity.

ISSUE OF POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

1) Is there a potential conflict of interest or a restriction in
assisting one of my clients in a variance application before
the Board? If so,

2) Can I represent a client in an application for permanent
variance with an administrative law judge presiding and acting
alone in hearing the variance proceeding?

3) With a client acting pro per or with legal representation by
an attorney at bar during a permanent variance proceeding, can
I appear as a witness?

4) Can I represent a client in an application for permanent
variance if I receive no compensation?

DISCUSSION

1) Is there a potential conflict of interest or a restriction in
assisting one of my clients in a variance application before
the Board?

"Assisting" may include many services including: following a letter of
authorization from the applicant, I apply for a permanent variance on
the client/applicant's behalf, conduct subsequent official
correspondence relating to the application, provide information in the
variance's evaluation process and appear as the sole representative of
the applicant at the Board's variance hearing, etc. Variances
allowed in cases of equivalent safety, are enabled by LC § 143.

2) Can I represent a client in an application for permanent
variance with an administrative law judge presiding and acting
alone in hearing the variance proceeding?

According to my knowledge, no variance hearings have ever been held
without a member of the Board acting as a panel member an a hearing
officer presiding. However, regulations (Title 8, California Code of
Regulations, Section 40.1(b)) allow for only a hearing officer to
hear the case of a variance. The hearing officer is an administrative
law judge. GC § 87406(d)(1) allows appearances before an
administrative law judge.

Does the term "appearance" include any of the activities other than
the hearing itself as described in 1) above, i.e., provide information
in the variance's evaluation process, etc.?

Letter of Advice for David W. Smith
January 30, 1991
Page 3

3)  With a client acting pro per or with legal representation by
    an attorney at bar during a permanent variance proceeding, can
    I appear as a witness?

This question should require no discussion.

4)  Can I represent a client in an application for permanent
    variance if I receive no compensation?

It is not my intent to direct my private practice towards an emphasis
of appearing before the Board. I am prepared to wait the one-year
period before most such representations, if any. However, one client
has already foregone other remedies with the belief that the best
remedy to a particular problem is by means of a permanent variance. I
would be willing to waive any compensation to assist this client in
this single matter.  I have not yet entered into what I believe would
be a conflict of interest and have not been compensated by that client
for appearing before the Board. GC § 87406(d)(1) restricts actions
"... for compensation...."

I am aware of the Government Code provisions for financial interest
and sources of income as requisites for a conflict of interest. Other
than the response to question number 4, you may presume these
requisites are exceeded in rendering your Letter of Advice.

Please let me know if you have questions. Mr. Keith Yamanaka, staff
counsel for the Board, (916)322-3640, may be able provide additional
information relative to the Board's activities. Thank you for
responding to this request.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]

David W. Smith

141 N. Terry Ave.
Clovis, CA 93612
(209)299-0501
DWS/bs

sections.question_synthetic: The requester, a former member of the Occupational Safety and Health Standards Board who resigned effective January 31, 1991, asks whether he can represent clients in variance applications before the Board and in what capacity, particularly regarding post-government service restrictions and potential conflicts of interest.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. The document contains background information, multiple numbered questions, and discussion sections but no formal FPPC conclusion or advice. The requester explicitly requests either a formal or informal letter of advice. Document quality has minor OCR issues but content is clear.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from David W. Smith, a departing Board member, seeking a formal letter of advice regarding potential conflicts of interest and post-government service restrictions related to representing clients in variance applications before the Board after his January 31, 1991 resignation.
